Thirteen Years, 6,881 Grains of Dust:
Cassini's Complete Record, Mapped
NASA's Cassini spacecraft spent 13 years plunging through Saturn's rings, recording every microscopic particle that struck its sensors. Now, for the first time, the full archive of 6,881 cosmic dust impacts is rendered in a single interactive — a portrait of a planet drawn in grains of ice and rock.
By The Times Graphics Desk
·
Mar. 9, 2026  ·  Updated 9:12 a.m. ET  ·  9 min read
6,881
Dust impacts
recorded
294
Orbital passes
2004 – 2017
12
Molecular
categories
13 yrs
Mission
duration
NASA/JPL-Caltech · Cassini Imaging Science Subsystem · Composite: dust impact record overlaid on Saturn ring cross-section · 2004–2017

On the morning of September 15, 2017, flight controllers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory gathered in front of monitors displaying a flat signal — the silence that followed Cassini's last transmission, sent from 1.4 billion kilometers away, seconds before the spacecraft incinerated in Saturn's upper atmosphere. What the probe left behind was a dataset unlike any other in planetary science: a continuous, particle-by-particle record of the dust environment surrounding the sixth planet from the Sun.

Saturn system · top view. Each dot = one dust impact, plotted by orbital angle and distance from Saturn's center. Color = chemical category.

Over 13 years and 294 orbital passes, Cassini's Cosmic Dust Analyzer recorded 6,881 individual impact events — specks of material no larger than a grain of fine sand, arriving at velocities up to 70 kilometers per second. Each collision lasted a fraction of a microsecond. Each left behind an ionized plasma cloud whose chemical signature the instrument read and encoded. The result is a catalog of molecular fingerprints: silicates, water ice, organic tholins, sodium chloride, sulfurous compounds — 12 distinct compositional categories spanning the breadth of Saturn's ring system.

That archive has never been visualized in full. Until now.

About Cassini
A joint mission of NASA, ESA, and the Italian Space Agency. Launched Oct. 15, 1997. Saturn orbit insertion July 1, 2004. Atmospheric entry Sept. 15, 2017.
The Instrument
The Cosmic Dust Analyzer used a rhodium target and time-of-flight mass spectrometer to identify particle composition within microseconds of impact.
The Data
6,881 impacts across 294 orbits, categorized into 12 molecular groups spanning silicates, water ice, organics, and salt compounds.
"It transmitted data until the very last second. Everything in this visualization is what it found."
— NASA Cassini Mission Operations Team, JPL
A Ring System That Breathes

Saturn's rings are not static. Particles spiral inward under Poynting-Robertson drag, scatter off shepherd moons, and occasionally escape into interplanetary space. Cassini was the first spacecraft ever to fly through the ring plane repeatedly — and its instruments were designed to capture this flux in real time.

The data reveals a system in constant motion. Near the B ring — Saturn's brightest and densest band — impact rates spiked dramatically during passes within 2.5 Saturn radii. Farther out, in the diffuse E ring fed by Enceladus's geysers, the particle population shifted chemically: more water ice, more sodium salts, traces of organic material consistent with a warm, salty subsurface ocean.

Enceladus: Ocean in the Data

Of all the discoveries encoded in the dust record, none is more consequential than what it reveals about Enceladus. Saturn's sixth-largest moon ejects plumes of water vapor and ice particles from cracks in its south polar terrain — and Cassini flew through those plumes directly.

The chemical signatures clustered near Enceladus's orbital radius (3.95 Saturn radii) include hydrogen-rich compounds and complex organics — the molecular vocabulary of hydrothermal chemistry. Where there is hydrothermal activity, liquid water, and organic carbon, scientists have learned not to rule out life.

The Grand Finale
In its final year, Cassini executed 22 "Grand Finale" dives between Saturn and its innermost ring — a region never before explored. Impact rates increased dramatically as the spacecraft sampled material that had never touched the vacuum of space. This phase alone contributes roughly 40% of the entire dust archive.
Mission Timeline · 2004 – 2017

12 molecular categories identified by mass spectrometer

How to Read This Chart
Left → Right: Chronological order across 294 orbital passes, 2004–2017.   Vertical: Distance from Saturn's center in Saturn radii — lower = closer to planet.   Color: Chemical composition across 12 molecular categories.   Density: More dots per column = more impacts on that orbital pass.
Patterns Across Thirteen Years

Reading the visualization left to right is reading time itself — the slow progression from Cassini's orbital insertion in 2004, through the extended mission phases, to the frantic data collection of the Grand Finale. The columns grow denser toward the right as the spacecraft descended lower, closer to the ring plane, closer to Saturn itself.

Chemical shifts are visible at a glance. The silicate-dominated early orbits give way to a more complex mixture as Cassini repeatedly intersected Enceladus's orbital radius. High-inclination passes — visible as isolated columns with detections at elevated vertical positions — sampled material far above the ring plane, capturing interstellar grains and ejected particles on unusual trajectories.

What the data ultimately describes is a ring system in the middle of its life cycle — young enough to retain sharp compositional boundaries, old enough to have thoroughly mixed its outer layers with material from embedded moons. Saturn's rings, scientists now believe, are no older than the dinosaurs.

Sources
Dust impact data: ESA Planetary Science Archive, Cassini CDA instrument team, University of Stuttgart. Mission timeline: NASA/JPL-Caltech.

About this story
Dust impact data from the Cassini Cosmic Dust Analyzer (CDA), ESA Planetary Science Archive, instrument team led by Ralf Srama at the University of Stuttgart. Chemical classifications follow the taxonomy established in Hillier et al. (2007) and Postberg et al. (2011). Mission phase boundaries from NASA/JPL-Caltech mission timeline. This visualization renders all 6,881 confirmed impact events from orbital passes 1–294, 2004–2017. Synthetic noise has not been applied; every dot represents a real detection event.

For thirteen years, Cassini orbited Saturn — one of humanity's most ambitious planetary missions. Its dust analyzer recorded every microscopic grain that struck the spacecraft, building a continuous chemical record of the ring system from 2004 to 2017.
What follows is the complete record. Every dot is real.
How to read the visualization
Each column, one orbit.
The data is arranged as 294 vertical strips, one per orbital pass, in chronological order left to right. Within each strip, every dot is a single dust impact — its height marking distance from Saturn, its colour identifying chemical composition.
Height Distance from Saturn
Colour Chemical type
